speaker: interviewer
text: We're speaking with Dr. Zev Kohn, in New York City. And this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my maternal grandparents, my grandmother and my grandfather. My name is Zev. And I got this name from this grandfather. When the picture was taken, and where, I have no idea. My mother gave me this picture after the war. And where-- how it was preserved during the war, by which one, which member of the family, I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Rose Bolkowski, and he's Zev Bolkowski. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father in the uniform that he was wearing as a student in high school. And this is 1913. So he was, at that time, 18 years old. The other people in this photograph are other students. But I don't know from where, how, or what school, or whatever. I-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Warsaw. And the name of my father is Samuel Kohn. It's a picture of my family and myself. It is most probably in [INAUDIBLE], which is a little resort-- resort area, at that time by train, probably half an hour from Warsaw. And I remember we used to go there in the ensuing summers, and take a cottage there, see pine trees there. I am here, sitting in the lap of my father. The year is probably 1931, and I'm one-year-old. Standing up from the right is my mother, middle is Zosia, who is the caretaker of the house. And she also was taking care when I was a little kid, of me. And to the extreme left is my sister, who is approximately 10 years older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is Helen Kohn. And my father is Samuel Kohn. It's a picture of myself at one-and-a-half years, probably taken in Warsaw. I look very sweet here, I think. Thank you. That's Zev Kohn. This is a family picture taken, probably, at-- in '35, in summer, '35, in Czestochowa, in the house of my grandmother, who is at the center of this picture, at the doorsteps of the house. To the extreme left is my sister. I am hiding behind my sister, at the age of five. And then next to me are my two cousins, Krzystoff and Adasz Standing behind them is their father, Stasiek, brother of my mother. Next to him, his-- his wife. In the middle, my grandmother. And then extreme left is Ann, my youngest sister of my mother. She perished in Czestochowa, ghetto. And he was killed during the 1939 war with the Germans. He was killed in Polish uniform. And that's it. This is picture of Targowa street, number 46, in Warsaw. The picture was taken right after the end of '45, 1945 war, by my cousin. And this is actually the gate the leads to the yard of the house where we used to live before the war. And I believe that the first-floor balcony on the left side is our balcony, and the adjoining window. This was a residence, our residence, until the outbreak of war in September of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you own the property, your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was-- in Warsaw, it was all rental. This is a picture of my older brother of my father. His name is Shimon Kohn. He was about 10 years older than my father. And during the war, he also fled Warsaw, and stayed with us in the eastern part of Poland. Between 1939 and '41, that part of Poland was occupied by Russians. He then stayed behind to join his wife and daughter, that is, my aunt and my cousin that stayed in Warsaw ghetto. But he never reached Warsaw. He was killed on the way back to Warsaw, most probably in the village where he resided, not far from Rowne. And I have only a suspicion-- it's not substantiated-- that he was burned alive by the Germans. He was a physician, an internist. Thank you. This is picture of my uncle, Daniel Kohn, younger brother of my father. He was a dentist in Konskie, in-- in Poland. And this is my aunt. I visited them in 1936. She died before the war. And he died of natural causes in Konskie ghetto. All his three children, that is my three cousins, survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Konskie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. But it had to be like, '34, '35. Oh, this is the picture taken at the 25th anniversary, wedding anniversary, of my parents. It was taken in Tehran, Persia, after our-- after our travels through-- through Russia, and such, and after we have been, so to speak, restored to normal health. I am in the middle. The year is 1942. And on my left is my sister. On my right, my mother. And behind me is my father, who was, at that time, a physician, and officer in Polish army. This is a identity card issued to the refugees, to my mother, in the year 1942, approximately less than a year after we came to then Palestine. So that was a short time after we emerged from the refugee camps and the initial encounters with-- with then, Palestine. The photograph itself might be still from the days of a refugee camp, rather than from actually-- rather than actually being taken after arrival to-- to Palestine. In 1942, my mother was approximately 49 years old. And the picture was either taken in Tel Aviv, or it was taken beforehand. I don't know. This is a picture of my parents, taken in Cologne, where they resided at that time, at the age of 65. They were both approximately the same age. And my father was going, then, into retirement. His name Samuel Kohn, [? Shmuel ?] Kohn. And my mother is Helen Kohn. That's about it. This is a tombstone of my paternal grandmother. The name is Kohn, as you can see on the photograph. I think there is a mistake, an error, in spelling in Hebrew, it's spelled with [INAUDIBLE] rather than with [INAUDIBLE]. In any case, her name is-- was Rose Kohn. And she died at the age of 50-some years, way before I was born, in Warsaw. The picture was taken after the war, by my cousin. It's a-- a photograph of the tombstone of my parents. The cemetery is in [INAUDIBLE], not far from Cologne. And they are both buried there. My-- my mother died approximately five years after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it-- when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken probably a year or so after my father's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me, after my mother's death, which would be approximately '79 or so. 1979, 1980, maybe. This is a photograph taken, obviously, many years back. It's a photograph of my son and myself. My son was, at that time-- it's hard to say, but probably three years old or so. He's my older son. His name is Daniel, after my uncle, Daniel. And that would put it around 1966, when I was approximately 36 years old. It's just to show one picture of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How-- how one picture out of all these pictures, of how people looked after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was taken? Or where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York-- in Scarsdale, New York.
